section.title: The Lord Is My Light and My Salvation
section.sectionRationale: A psalm of David that moves from confident trust, verses 1-6, into urgent lament and plea, verses 7-13, before closing with an exhortation to wait on the LORD. Its turn from bold confidence to desperate prayer, and back to hope, models the honest rhythm of real faith under real pressure.
section.thesis: David's confidence that the LORD is his light and salvation does not remove his fear and need, but drives him to seek God's face all the more urgently, resting his hope on the certainty that he will see God's goodness in the land of the living.
section.frameNote: Four movements: confident trust that the LORD is light and salvation (1-3); the one thing asked, to dwell in God's house and be exalted above enemies (4-6); an urgent cry for mercy and God's presence despite even parental abandonment (7-10); and a prayer for guidance from false witnesses, closing in confident hope and exhortation (11-14).

units.label: The LORD is my light and my salvation

verses.text: The LORD is my {{or|light}} and my salvation, whom shall I fear? The LORD is the stronghold of my life, whom shall I dread?
When the wicked came upon me to devour my flesh, my enemies and foes stumbled and fell.
Though an army encamps around me, my heart will not fear; though a war breaks out against me, I will keep my trust.

units.label: One thing I have asked of the LORD

verses.text: One thing I have asked of the LORD; this is what I desire: to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze on the beauty of the LORD and seek Him in His temple.
For in the day of trouble He will hide me in His shelter; He will conceal me under the cover of His tent; He will set me high upon a rock.
Then my head will be held high above my enemies around me. At His tabernacle I will offer sacrifices with shouts of joy; I will sing and make music to the LORD.

units.label: Hide not Your face from me

verses.text: Hear, O LORD, my voice when I call; be merciful and answer me.
My heart said, "Seek His face." Your face, O LORD, I will {{baqash|seek}}.

Hide not Your face from me, nor turn away Your servant in anger. You have been my helper; do not leave me or forsake me, 
O God of my salvation.
Though my father and mother forsake me, the LORD will receive me.

Teach me Your way, O LORD, and lead me on a level path, 
because of my oppressors.

Do not hand me over to the will of my foes, for false witnesses rise up against me, 
breathing out violence.

Still I am certain to see the goodness of the LORD 
in the land of the living.

Wait patiently for the LORD; be strong and {{chazaq|courageous}}. 
Wait patiently for the LORD!

or.gloss: Light, the first thing God spoke into being, Genesis 1:3, and a common picture of God's saving presence dispelling darkness, danger, and fear. David names the LORD Himself as his light, not merely a giver of light.

baqash.gloss: To seek, search for, desire earnestly. David's whole heart echoes God's own invitation back to Him, turning command into desire, 'Seek His face,' answered by 'Your face, O LORD, I will seek.'

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, firm, courageous, often used in commands to take heart before a daunting task, as with Joshua, Joshua 1:6-9. The psalm's closing exhortation urges this same strengthened resolve while waiting on the LORD.

basic.context: David opens with bold confidence, the LORD as his light and salvation, whom he need not fear even facing an army. But by verse 7 the tone shifts into urgent plea, asking God not to hide His face, not to forsake him, even if his own parents were to abandon him. The psalm closes by returning to confident hope, that David will see God's goodness in the land of the living, and a call to wait patiently and courageously on the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm's shift from bold confidence to desperate lament and back again should be read not as contradiction but as the honest shape of real faith, which does not pretend fear away but brings it directly to God, anchored in His character rather than in the absence of danger.
basic.originalLanguages: David calls the LORD his or, light, not merely a source of light but light itself. His deepest desire, baqash, to seek God's face, answers God's own invitation, and the psalm closes urging chazaq, strength and courage, while waiting on the LORD rather than looking to circumstances for confidence.
basic.typology: David's one request, to dwell in the house of the LORD and gaze on His beauty, verse 4, anticipates the greater and permanent dwelling Christ provides for His people in His Father's house, John 14:2-3. His hope in verse 10, that the LORD receives him even if father and mother forsake him, anticipates the full adoption believers receive in Christ, Romans 8:15.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true light, John 8:12, of whom David's confession is a shadow, and the one who grants not merely a visit to God's house but a permanent, prepared place there, John 14:2-3. Where David could only ask to seek God's face, believers now behold the glory of God fully in the face of Jesus Christ, 2 Corinthians 4:6. And David's confidence that the LORD receives him even if parents forsake him is fulfilled in the full adoption as sons and daughters that comes through Christ, Romans 8:15, Ephesians 1:5.
basic.god: God is shown as both refuge and answer to fear, one whose presence, not the absence of enemies or danger, is the true security, and one who receives the abandoned and forsaken even when the closest human relationships fail.
basic.crossReferences: John 8:12 shows Jesus as the light of the world, fulfilling David's confession of the LORD as his light. John 14:2-3 shows Christ preparing a permanent place in His Father's house, the fulfillment of David's one request in verse 4. Romans 8:15 shows believers receiving the Spirit of adoption, sons and daughters, whom no earthly abandonment can undo.
basic.oneStory: David's confidence that the LORD is his light and salvation carries him through fear, betrayal, and even the possibility of parental abandonment, into a settled hope of seeing God's goodness. That hope is fulfilled completely in Jesus Christ, the true light of the world, who receives the forsaken as adopted children and prepares an everlasting place for them in His Father's house.

context.literary: A psalm of David combining confident trust, verses 1-6, with urgent lament and plea, verses 7-13, unified by the psalm's driving desire to seek and dwell in God's presence, and closing with a call to wait patiently and courageously on the LORD.
context.historical: Traditionally attributed to David amid conflict with enemies and false accusation, reflecting both the confidence appropriate to God's anointed king and the vulnerability of a man surrounded by real danger and betrayal, even within his own family circle.
context.canonical: Belongs to the wider family of psalms of trust and lament combined, showing the Psalter's pattern of holding bold confession and honest need together, later echoed in the New Testament's call to approach God's throne of grace with confidence in time of need, Hebrews 4:16.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To express confident trust in the LORD as light and salvation while honestly bringing fear, danger, and the threat of abandonment before Him, and to teach that waiting on the LORD, not the removal of trouble, is the ground of courage.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own experience of both confidence and urgent need; it functions prescriptively as a pattern for believers to hold bold trust and honest lament together rather than choosing one over the other.

debates.question: Does the marked shift in tone between verses 1-6 (confident trust) and verses 7-13 (urgent lament) suggest this is a single unified psalm, or possibly two originally separate compositions later joined?

views.label: This is a single, unified psalm reflecting the normal movement of genuine faith, which can hold bold confidence and urgent need together without contradiction, since real trust in God does not eliminate fear but processes it through prayer
views.case: Many psalms of trust move between confidence and plea within a single composition, and the psalm's own internal logic, verse 6's worship leading into verse 7's cry, reads naturally as one continuous prayer rather than two unrelated pieces stitched together.

views.label: Some scholars propose the abrupt shift in tone and address suggests an original combination of two separate psalms, a hymn of confidence and a lament, joined for liturgical use
views.case: The change from third-person confidence about the LORD to urgent, direct second-person plea is more pronounced than the gradual movement typically seen within a single psalm, leading some interpreters to see two originally distinct compositions.

debates.bearing: Whether one composition or two joined together, the canonical form teaches the same truth: confident trust and honest, urgent lament belong together in a life of faith, and neither should be read as canceling out the other.

study.originalLanguages: David names the LORD his or, light itself, not merely its giver, and his deepest baqash, seeking, answers God's own invitation to seek His face; the psalm closes urging chazaq, strength and courage, while waiting on the LORD rather than on changed circumstances.

typology.type: David's one request, to dwell in the LORD's house and gaze on His beauty, verse 4
typology.fulfillment: The permanent place Christ prepares for His people in His Father's house, John 14:2-3.

typology.type: The LORD receiving David even if father and mother forsake him, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: The full adoption as sons and daughters that believers receive through Christ, Romans 8:15, Ephesians 1:5.

study.christ: Jesus is the light of the world, John 8:12, the true fulfillment of David's confession in verse 1. David's one great request, to dwell in God's house and gaze on His beauty, is answered fully by Christ, who prepares a permanent place in His Father's house for all who trust Him, John 14:2-3, and in whose face believers now behold the glory of God, 2 Corinthians 4:6. And where David hoped the LORD would receive him even in parental abandonment, Christ secures the full adoption of believers as sons and daughters who can never be forsaken, Romans 8:15.
study.god: God is shown as refuge and light whose presence, not the absence of danger, grounds real courage, and as one who receives and keeps the abandoned close even when the nearest human relationships fail.

crossReferences.note: Jesus declares Himself the light of the world, the fulfillment of David's confession that the LORD is his light.
Christ prepares a permanent place in His Father's house, fulfilling David's one great request in verse 4.
Believers receive the Spirit of adoption as sons and daughters, fulfilling David's hope of being received even if forsaken by parents.

study.oneStory: David's confidence that the LORD is his light and salvation carries him through fear and even the threat of abandonment into settled hope of seeing God's goodness in the land of the living. That hope reaches its fullness in Jesus Christ, the true light of the world, who receives the forsaken as adopted children and secures for them an everlasting place in His Father's house.

sermon.title: Repose: The Power and Glory
sermon.why: Keller preached this sermon on Psalm 27:1-14 as part of the series The Prayer of Prayers: Exploring Jesus' Model, drawing out how the psalm's movement from confidence to urgent plea models genuine, Christ-shaped prayer.

quiz.q: What does David call the LORD in verse 1, and what effect does this have on his fear?

options.text: His light and his salvation, the stronghold of his life, removing the grounds for fear or dread.
options.note: David explicitly asks 'whom shall I fear' and 'whom shall I dread' on the basis of these titles for the LORD.

options.text: His judge, leading him to dread God's coming verdict.
options.note: The verse expresses confidence and removal of fear, not dread of judgment.

options.text: A helper among others, one source of confidence alongside his own strength.
options.note: Close, since God is indeed a helper, but the verse makes the LORD the sole ground of fearlessness, not merely one source of confidence among others.

options.text: His silent observer, giving no comfort at all.
options.note: The verse describes an active, comforting relationship, the opposite of silent, uninvolved observation.

quiz.q: What is the 'one thing' David asks of the LORD in verse 4?

options.text: To dwell in the house of the LORD all his days, gazing on His beauty and seeking Him in His temple.
options.note: This is David's single, focused, deepest desire above all others.

options.text: To defeat all his enemies in battle.
options.note: While enemies are mentioned, the singular request of verse 4 concerns dwelling with God, not military victory.

options.text: To receive great wealth and possessions.
options.note: The request is entirely about God's presence, not material gain.

options.text: To spend time in worship generally, wherever it happens to occur.
options.note: Close, since worship is central, but verse 4 specifically names dwelling in the LORD's house and temple, a particular place of God's presence, not worship in general.

quiz.q: What striking claim does David make in verse 10?

options.text: Even if his father and mother forsake him, the LORD will receive him.
options.note: This verse pictures God's care as more reliable than even the closest human family bonds.

options.text: His parents will never forsake him.
options.note: The verse is a hypothetical concession, 'though,' not a promise about his parents' behavior.

options.text: Human family will always ultimately be replaced by God's people.
options.note: Close in spirit, since God's reception is central, but the verse's specific point is God's personal reception of David himself, not a statement about replacing family with community.

options.text: He no longer needs any human relationships.
options.note: The verse addresses the worst case of abandonment, not a rejection of human relationships generally.

quiz.q: What does David ask for in verse 11, and why?

options.text: To be taught God's way and led on a level path, because of his oppressors.
options.note: David explicitly ties his request for guidance to the pressure of those opposing him.

options.text: To be given a new kingdom entirely.
options.note: The request is for guidance and a level path, not a new kingdom.

options.text: To have his oppressors immediately destroyed.
options.note: Close, since the oppressors are named as the reason for the request, but verse 11 specifically asks for guidance and a level path, not the immediate destruction of the oppressors.

options.text: To be left alone by God and others.
options.note: This is nearly the opposite of the request; David is asking for God's active teaching and leading, not solitude.

quiz.q: What is David's closing exhortation in verse 14?

options.text: To wait patiently for the LORD, being strong and courageous.
options.note: The psalm closes with a repeated call to patient, courageous waiting on the LORD.

options.text: To give up hope if deliverance does not come quickly.
options.note: The exhortation is the opposite, urging patient, courageous waiting rather than giving up.

options.text: To be strong on his own initiative, without reference to the LORD.
options.note: Close, since strength and courage are named, but verse 14 ties both explicitly to waiting for the LORD, not self-generated resolve.

options.text: To seek comfort in wealth instead of prayer.
options.note: The explicit exhortation concerns waiting on the LORD, not seeking comfort elsewhere.

questions: David calls the LORD his light and salvation, removing grounds for fear even facing an army. What circumstance in your life currently tempts you most toward fear, and how might naming God as your light change that?

David's one great request is simply to dwell in God's presence and gaze on His beauty. If you could ask God for only one thing, would it be this, and if not, what does that reveal about your deepest desires?

David's confidence in verses 1-6 gives way to urgent, almost desperate pleading by verse 7. Where in your own life do you need to bring bold trust and honest desperation to God together, rather than pretending only one is true?

David says the LORD will receive him even if his father and mother forsake him. Who or what have you relied on for security that could fail you, and how does knowing God's reception is more certain change that reliance?

The psalm ends by urging patient, courageous waiting on the LORD rather than giving up or taking control. What are you currently waiting on God for, and what would courageous patience look like this week?

translationNotes.note: Stronghold can also be rendered refuge, both pointing to a place of safety rather than merely strength; the BSB's dash-marked pauses in this verse are rendered here as commas for consistency.
Seek His face was likely a known call to worship or prayer; David's response turns the command into personal, wholehearted desire.
The land of the living can refer to this present earthly life or carry a broader hope beyond death, consistent with the Psalter's developing hope of resurrection.
